scenario: A useDebouncedValue hook used in a search input to delay expensive API calls
submitter_context: Small hook to debounce input values. Replaces 3 duplicated debounce implementations across the codebase.
looks_reasonable_because: Uses a ref to avoid stale closures on the callback
review_summary: The effect drops `delay` from its dependency array, so changing delay won't take effect until the next value change. More importantly, the callback ref pattern here is broken because the ref is mutated during render, which is unsafe in concurrent rendering. This will produce surprising behavior under Strict Mode and React 19's concurrent features.

issues_found.title: Missing `delay` dependency
issues_found.explanation: If the caller passes a dynamic delay (e.g. user-controlled slider), changing delay won't re-arm the timer until value changes too. The eslint react-hooks rule would flag this; it appears to have been silenced or ignored.
issues_found.root_cause: Author optimized for the common case where delay is static.
issues_found.fix: Add delay to the dependency array: `[value, delay]`.
issues_found.verification: Render with a delay prop that changes; verify the effective debounce reflects the new delay.

issues_found.title: Ref mutation during render
issues_found.explanation: Assigning to a ref during the render phase violates the rules of React. Under concurrent rendering, a render can be discarded and re-run, but the ref write persists, leading to inconsistent state. React 19 with Strict Mode double-invokes function bodies, making this a real source of bugs.
issues_found.root_cause: Common pattern that predates concurrent React; needs to move into an effect.
issues_found.fix: Wrap in `useEffect(() => { callbackRef.current = onChange; });` (no deps), or better: use `useEffectEvent` (experimental) or accept that the callback is captured per-effect.
issues_found.verification: Enable Strict Mode and confirm the callback fired matches the value passed.

issues_found.title: Side-effect callback fires even when value is unchanged after rapid edits
issues_found.explanation: Because setDebounced is followed by the callback in the same setTimeout, the callback fires every time the timer resolves, even if value happens to equal the previous debounced value (e.g. user types and reverts). For an API call this means redundant requests.
issues_found.root_cause: Coupled state update and side effect.
issues_found.fix: Use a useEffect on `debounced` to fire the callback; or compare against previous debounced before invoking.
issues_found.verification: Type 'a', delete to '', type 'a' again within delay window; assert callback fires only once for 'a'.

issues_found.title: Two responsibilities in one hook
issues_found.explanation: Combining `debounced value` and `onChange callback` mixes derived state with a side-effect bus. Consumers usually want one or the other; offering both increases surface area and breeds bugs like #3.
issues_found.fix: Drop the callback param. Consumers can run `useEffect(() => { fetch(debounced); }, [debounced]);`.
issues_found.verification: Code review: confirm callers don't need the dual API.

issues_that_arent_issues: The generic `<T>` and default `delay = 300` are fine. clearTimeout in the cleanup correctly cancels in-flight debounces.
broader_pattern: `useRef`-as-mailbox during render is a code smell in React 18+ and an outright bug in React 19 concurrent rendering. Hooks that bundle state + callback often hide bugs; prefer pure-derived hooks and let consumers compose their own effects.
what_makes_this_review_senior: The reviewer catches the obvious missing dependency but also recognizes the ref-during-render antipattern as concurrent-rendering-unsafe (not just a lint warning). The structural critique about combining derived state with callbacks shows architectural taste that prevents the next bug.

scenario: A global toast notification system with hooks and a portal-based renderer
submitter_context: Replaces ad-hoc inline toasts with a centralized API. Supports auto-dismiss and manual dismissal.
review_summary: Three real leaks: subscribers re-registered every render, setTimeout handles outlive unmount, and listeners hold closures over stale state. The document.body access also breaks SSR. This will leak memory in long-lived sessions and crash Next.js apps.

issues_found.title: Listener registered on every render
issues_found.explanation: Without a dependency array the effect runs after every render. Each run adds a NEW listener function and the cleanup removes the PREVIOUS one. Under React 19 Strict Mode the double-invocation amplifies the churn. If a render throws between add and cleanup the listener leaks permanently.
issues_found.fix: Change to useEffect(() => {...}, []).
issues_found.verification: Log listeners.size; should stay at 1 over many toasts.

issues_found.title: Timer keeps running after viewport unmounts
issues_found.explanation: If ToastViewport unmounts (route change, conditional render), pending setTimeouts still fire, mutate the module array, and call listeners that may be GCed or that capture stale state. In Next.js the listener can call setState on an unmounted tree and trigger warnings.
issues_found.root_cause: No way to cancel pending dismissals.
issues_found.fix: Track timers by id; clear them when toast is removed manually OR when viewport unmounts. Or move the timer into the React tree (per-toast component with useEffect + setTimeout + cleanup).
issues_found.verification: Mount/unmount viewport while toasts are pending; assert no console errors.

issues_found.title: SSR / hydration crash
issues_found.explanation: createPortal(..., document.body) runs during render. In SSR (Next.js, Remix) document is undefined, blowing up the server render. Even client-only setups can fail during hydration if body isn't mounted yet.
issues_found.fix: Gate with a mounted state: render null on first render, then portal after useEffect flips mounted = true.
issues_found.verification: Run a server render; should not throw.

issues_found.title: State leaks across tests and HMR
issues_found.explanation: nextId and toasts survive across hot-reloads in dev and persist across tests in jsdom. Tests that don't manually reset will see leakage. In serverless Node this state is shared across requests.
issues_found.root_cause: Global mutable state with no reset hook.
issues_found.fix: Expose a reset() for tests. For SSR safety, scope state to a React provider or per-request store.
issues_found.verification: Run two tests sequentially; second should start with zero toasts without explicit reset.

issues_found.title: Initial state captures module array reference
issues_found.explanation: useState(toasts) reads the live module array. Concurrent rendering may discard the initial render but the reference is already shared, leading to surprising tearing under heavy load.
issues_found.fix: useState<Toast[]>(() => [...toasts]) to snapshot. Better: useSyncExternalStore.
issues_found.verification: N/A; defensive.

issues_that_arent_issues: The Set-of-listeners pattern itself is fine. Tailwind classes look correct. The Toast shape is reasonable.
broader_pattern: Module-level stores look simple but accumulate hidden lifetime bugs: SSR unsafety, test leakage, HMR persistence, timer leaks. Libraries like Zustand/Jotai exist precisely to formalize this. For toasts, Sonner/Radix Toast own timers per-component, avoiding all four bugs.
what_makes_this_review_senior: The reviewer doesn't just point at the missing dep array. They trace through SSR, HMR, test isolation, and timer lifetime as distinct failure modes. The fix uses useSyncExternalStore (the correct React 18+ primitive for external stores) and adds a test-reset hook, signaling someone who has shipped this kind of system in production.

scenario: An autocomplete search input that fetches suggestions as the user types
submitter_context: Adds autocomplete to the global header search. Debounced + uses fetch + AbortController.
looks_reasonable_because: Cleanup function runs on q change
review_summary: The cancellation looks right but the finally block flips loading=false on aborted requests, causing UI flicker when typing fast. Also q is interpolated into the URL without encoding (XSS-on-self if special chars, and broken queries with &/=), and the empty-q path leaves a stale loading=true if it follows a real query. Plus the catch swallows AbortError as a real error.

issues_found.title: Loading state toggled on aborted requests
issues_found.explanation: When the user types fast, each prior effect aborts. The aborted fetch's finally still runs (after the await throws AbortError), calling setLoading(false) on a component that has just started a NEW request and set loading=true. Result: the spinner flickers off then on. Worse, the LATEST request's setResults can be raced by an OLDER aborted request's finally if microtask ordering shifts.
issues_found.root_cause: finally runs even on AbortError; no check for signal.aborted.
issues_found.fix: Inside finally (or before each setState), check if (ac.signal.aborted) return.
issues_found.verification: Type 5 chars rapidly; spinner should remain steady, not flicker.

issues_found.title: Unencoded query parameter
issues_found.explanation: If q contains &, =, #, or whitespace, the URL is malformed. q='a&b=evil' becomes /api/suggest?q=a&b=evil, injecting a second param. This is also a vector if server logging/echoing happens unfiltered.
issues_found.root_cause: Template string interpolation instead of URLSearchParams.
issues_found.fix: Use URLSearchParams or encodeURIComponent: fetch(`/api/suggest?${new URLSearchParams({ q })}`).
issues_found.verification: Test with q='a&debug=1'; server should receive q as 'a&debug=1', not split.

issues_found.title: Early return leaks prior loading state
issues_found.explanation: If results were loading and the user clears the input, loading stays true forever because the effect early-returns without setLoading(false).
issues_found.fix: setLoading(false) on the early-return branch.
issues_found.verification: Type 'abc', delete to ''; loading should be false.

issues_found.title: Catches AbortError as a real error
issues_found.explanation: AbortController throws DOMException name=AbortError on abort. Logging this floods console (and any error tracker like Sentry) with non-errors on every keystroke.
issues_found.fix: if ((e as Error).name === 'AbortError') return;.
issues_found.verification: Sentry should not record abort events.

issues_found.title: setTimeout-based debounce inside effect
issues_found.explanation: Works, but couples debounce to the effect lifecycle. A first character will fire after 200ms; if q goes empty during that window, an empty query may still race in. Prefer a debounced value via useDebouncedValue.
issues_found.fix: Use a separate useDebouncedValue(q, 200) and have this effect depend on debounced.
issues_found.verification: Type 'a' then backspace within 200ms; no request fires.

issues_found.title: Treats any response as JSON
issues_found.explanation: If the server returns 500 with HTML, res.json() throws and falls into the catch. Better to check res.ok and surface to the user.
issues_found.fix: if (!res.ok) throw new Error(`HTTP ${res.status}`);.
issues_found.verification: Server returns 500; UI shows an error state, not silent.

issues_that_arent_issues: AbortController usage itself is correct. useEffect deps are correct. Using state for results is fine.
broader_pattern: Async-in-useEffect has four canonical bugs: cancellation, stale-state writes, error filtering, and loading-flag hygiene. The community now leans on react-query/SWR specifically because hand-rolled implementations almost always miss one of these. If this is going into the codebase, consider whether react-query already solves it.
what_makes_this_review_senior: The reviewer goes beyond 'add AbortController' (which is already there) to identify the subtler races: finally-on-abort, the empty-input loading leak, and AbortError pollution of error tracking. They also flag URL encoding as a correctness AND injection concern, and end with the architectural recommendation to lean on react-query rather than maintaining yet another bespoke async hook.

scenario: A useLocalStorage hook used to persist user preferences across reloads
submitter_context: Replaces ad-hoc localStorage calls scattered across the app. Generic, typed, with a default value.
review_summary: This will crash on the server (ReferenceError: localStorage is not defined) and even if you guard that, it will hydration-mismatch: the server renders the default value but the client immediately shows the stored value, causing React 19 to throw a hydration error. Also no cross-tab sync, no JSON parse safety, no key change handling.

issues_found.title: Crashes during SSR
issues_found.explanation: In Next.js/Remix the function body runs on the server where localStorage doesn't exist. The lazy initializer throws ReferenceError, breaking the entire server render.
issues_found.fix: Check typeof window !== 'undefined' in the initializer; return initial on server.
issues_found.verification: SSR a page using this hook; should not throw.

issues_found.location: Server vs client initial value divergence
issues_found.title: Hydration mismatch warning / error
issues_found.explanation: Even with a typeof window guard, the server returns initial, but the first client render reads the stored value. React 19 treats this as a hydration mismatch and may discard the entire client tree, also logging an error.
issues_found.root_cause: useState's initial value differs between SSR and CSR.
issues_found.fix: Always render initial on first render, then update to the stored value inside useEffect (post-hydration). Track a mounted state if you must avoid the brief flicker, or use useSyncExternalStore with a getServerSnapshot.
issues_found.verification: In Next.js dev, navigate to the page; no hydration error in console.

issues_found.title: Missing functional updater overload
issues_found.explanation: useState supports setValue(prev => next). This wrapper only allows direct values, so any consumer doing setTheme(t => t === 'light' ? 'dark' : 'light') silently breaks (TS error or stale closure).
issues_found.fix: Type as Dispatch<SetStateAction<T>> and pass through.
issues_found.verification: Type-check: callers using updater function compile.

issues_found.location: useEffect writes on every value change
issues_found.title: No cross-tab sync; no `storage` event listener
issues_found.explanation: If the user opens two tabs, changing theme in one doesn't update the other. localStorage emits a storage event for that purpose, but this hook ignores it.
issues_found.fix: Subscribe to window.addEventListener('storage', handler) and update state when key matches.
issues_found.verification: Two tabs open; change in one updates the other within ~50ms.

issues_found.title: Unsafe JSON parse
issues_found.explanation: If localStorage was written by an older code version with a different schema, or corrupted, JSON.parse throws and the hook never recovers. Production crash from a string nobody can explain.
issues_found.fix: try { return JSON.parse(stored) as T; } catch { return initial; }.
issues_found.verification: Set localStorage to an invalid string; hook falls back to initial.

issues_found.title: Changing key doesn't migrate value
issues_found.explanation: If a caller changes the key prop, useEffect writes the current value under the new key but doesn't read from it first. Behavior is surprising.
issues_found.fix: Document that key should be static; warn in dev if it changes. Or read on key change.
issues_found.verification: Code review.

issues_that_arent_issues: Tuple return shape is fine. JSON serialization for non-primitive T is reasonable.
broader_pattern: localStorage hooks are a hydration minefield. The right primitive is useSyncExternalStore with both getSnapshot and getServerSnapshot, which formalizes the SSR/CSR divergence problem instead of papering over it. Many open-source 'useLocalStorage' implementations have one or more of these bugs; choose carefully.
what_makes_this_review_senior: The reviewer recognizes the SSR crash AND the more subtle hydration-mismatch even after the guard is added; many engineers stop after fixing the typeof check. They also catch the missing functional-updater type (a real ergonomics regression), the JSON.parse crash, and propose useSyncExternalStore as the structurally correct primitive.

scenario: A custom DropdownMenu with keyboard navigation, used in app header
submitter_context: Replaces the third-party Menu library to drop a 12kb dep. Supports keyboard navigation per WAI-ARIA.
review_summary: This menu fails almost every a11y requirement: no keyboard navigation (Tab moves OUT of the menu, not between items), no Esc to close, no roles/aria, items are <div> not <button> so screen readers can't operate them, and focus isn't restored to the trigger on close. Also the outside-click handler closes the menu before button clicks can register on some browsers.

issues_found.title: Menu items not focusable or operable by keyboard
issues_found.explanation: <div onClick> is not focusable, has no implicit role, and is invisible to screen readers as an interactive element. Keyboard users cannot reach or activate items.
issues_found.fix: Use <button type="button" role="menuitem"> or set role="menuitem" with tabIndex={-1} and handle keydown explicitly. Manage roving tabindex so arrow keys move focus.
issues_found.verification: Tab into trigger, press Enter to open, ArrowDown moves through items, Enter selects, Esc closes.

issues_found.title: No ARIA roles
issues_found.explanation: Missing role="menu" on the popup, aria-haspopup/aria-expanded on the trigger, aria-controls linking trigger to menu. Screen readers announce this as 'button' with no indication of a menu.
issues_found.fix: Add role="menu", aria-orientation="vertical". Trigger: aria-haspopup="menu", aria-expanded={open}, aria-controls={menuId}.
issues_found.verification: VoiceOver/NVDA announces 'menu' and item count.

issues_found.title: Race with menu-item click
issues_found.explanation: document click fires during the BUBBLE phase, AFTER the item onClick already toggled state. Usually fine, but if the menu re-renders due to onSelect side-effects and the item is unmounted, the document click handler sees e.target as a node no longer in ref.current and closes redundantly (harmless) - BUT if you switch to mousedown (a common 'fix'), it fires BEFORE click and cancels the click entirely.
issues_found.fix: Keep listener on click (bubble) and ensure items use onClick (not onMouseDown). Document this constraint.
issues_found.verification: Click item; onSelect runs exactly once; menu closes.

issues_found.title: No Escape-to-close, no focus restoration
issues_found.explanation: Escape should close the menu. Focus must return to the trigger button after close (otherwise keyboard users are stranded at document.body). This is a WCAG requirement for menus.
issues_found.root_cause: Missing keydown handler and trigger ref.
issues_found.fix: Add keydown on the menu container handling Escape -> setOpen(false) + triggerRef.current?.focus().
issues_found.verification: Open menu, press Esc; focus returns to trigger.

issues_found.title: Tab leaves the menu
issues_found.explanation: When the menu opens, Tab moves to the next tabbable element on the page rather than cycling through items. ARIA menu pattern uses arrow keys for navigation, not Tab, but Tab should at least close the menu.
issues_found.fix: On open, focus the first item. ArrowDown/Up navigate. Tab closes the menu and moves to next focusable element after the trigger.
issues_found.verification: Manual keyboard test.

issues_found.title: `items` requires unique stable ids
issues_found.explanation: No runtime check; if duplicate ids, React key warning fires in dev only. Document the contract.
issues_found.fix: Document; optionally add a dev-only Set check.
issues_found.verification: N/A.

issues_found.title: Cost-benefit
issues_found.explanation: Radix Menu is ~15kb minified+gzipped and ships all the a11y above for free. Saving 12kb at the cost of broken a11y is a net loss. Confirm the trade-off with design + a11y before merging.
issues_found.fix: Reconsider. If you must roll your own, base it on Radix's headless primitives.
issues_found.verification: Re-evaluate.

issues_that_arent_issues: Outside-click pattern itself is fine. useEffect deps are correct.
broader_pattern: Custom dropdowns are an a11y graveyard. The WAI-ARIA Authoring Practices menu pattern is ~200 lines of keyboard handling done right. If the team is dropping a library to save bundle, mandate that the replacement passes axe + manual keyboard testing before merge.
what_makes_this_review_senior: The reviewer treats a11y as a first-class shipping requirement, not a follow-up. They also raise the cost-benefit of dropping a library: 12kb of bundle is not worth shipping an inaccessible menu. The fix implements the actual ARIA menu pattern (arrow keys, roving tabindex, Esc, focus restoration, Home/End), not just a couple of attributes.

scenario: A ThemeProvider that exposes the current theme + setter via React Context
submitter_context: Centralizes theme state. Subscribes to system color-scheme changes. Used near the root of the app.
looks_reasonable_because: useEffect adds + removes the mq listener
Generic enough to drop near root
review_summary: Three rerender problems compound: the context value is a new object every render (cascading rerenders across every consumer), splitting read/write into one context forces components that only call setTheme to rerender on theme changes, and the initial 'light' value causes a hydration flicker (FOUC) before the effect runs. For a near-root provider this is a real performance hit.

issues_found.title: Context value object identity changes every render
issues_found.explanation: On every render of ThemeProvider a fresh {theme, setTheme} object is created. Every consumer using useContext rerenders even if neither theme nor setTheme actually changed (e.g. a parent rerenders for unrelated reasons). At app root this can cascade to thousands of components.
issues_found.fix: useMemo the value: const value = useMemo(() => ({ theme, setTheme }), [theme]). setTheme from useState is already stable.
issues_found.verification: Profiler shows consumers don't rerender when ThemeProvider rerenders for unrelated reasons.

issues_found.title: Components that only set the theme still rerender on theme change
issues_found.explanation: A ThemeToggle component that only calls setTheme has no business rerendering when theme changes elsewhere - but because both come from one context, it does. Standard pattern: split into two contexts (state + dispatcher).
issues_found.fix: Create ThemeStateContext (value: theme) and ThemeSetContext (value: setTheme). Toggle components subscribe only to the setter.
issues_found.verification: Profiler: changing theme rerenders consumers of state, NOT consumers of setter only.

issues_found.title: Theme flicker (FOUC) on first paint
issues_found.explanation: Server (or initial CSR) renders 'light'. After hydration the effect reads the media query and sets to 'dark', triggering a re-render and a visible flash. For a dark-mode-preferring user this is jarring.
issues_found.root_cause: Initial state can't read window at SSR.
issues_found.fix: Use useSyncExternalStore with getServerSnapshot returning a sensible default, OR inject a sync script in the document head that sets a class on <html> before React hydrates (the standard Next.js/Theme-UI approach).
issues_found.verification: Load with prefers-color-scheme: dark; no light flash on first paint.

issues_found.title: Setter overwrites user choice
issues_found.explanation: If the user manually calls setTheme('light') but their OS later switches to dark mode, the mq handler clobbers the user's choice. Theme providers usually layer: user choice > system > default.
issues_found.fix: Track theme = 'system' | 'light' | 'dark'. The mq handler only updates the resolved theme; user explicit choice persists in localStorage and wins.
issues_found.verification: Set theme manually, change OS theme, manual choice persists.

issues_found.title: Throw text doesn't aid debugging
issues_found.explanation: 'useTheme outside ThemeProvider' is correct but doesn't say which component. In a deep tree this is hard to track. Use Error with component name from a stack-aware DevTools display name.
issues_found.fix: Include hook name and recommend wrapping with <ThemeProvider> in the message.
issues_found.verification: N/A.

issues_that_arent_issues: Use of matchMedia + change event is the correct API. Cleanup is present. Throwing for missing provider is the right shape.
broader_pattern: Context performance issues are usually fixed by (1) memoizing the value, (2) splitting read/write contexts, and (3) using useSyncExternalStore for external sources. Theme flicker specifically is the canonical case for a tiny synchronous bootstrapping script in <head> before hydration.
what_makes_this_review_senior: The reviewer separates three distinct rerender causes (object identity, combined context, system-vs-user precedence) instead of lumping them as 'context is slow'. They also flag FOUC and prescribe the canonical solution (head script), which is something you only suggest if you've debugged it in production.

scenario: A virtualized list that scrolls smoothly through 10k items, also supports highlight-and-scroll-to
submitter_context: In-house virtualizer replacing a custom react-window wrapper. Adds scrollToIndex.
looks_reasonable_because: Listens to scroll on the container
review_summary: setState on every scroll causes the whole component (and parent if memoized incorrectly) to rerender 60+ times/sec, dropping frames during fast scroll. The transform target is positioned absolutely incorrectly (not absolute), so the offset transform creates a stacking context but doesn't fix the visible items in the scroll viewport. Also scrollToIndex retriggers if the same index is set twice (e.g. user clicks same item) and the smooth scroll fights the user's input.

issues_found.title: Setting state on every scroll event blocks the main thread
issues_found.explanation: Scroll fires at frame rate. Each setState schedules a render. For 10k items the diff is small but the React commit + style recalc still costs ~2-5ms per frame. On low-end devices this drops scroll FPS noticeably.
issues_found.fix: Throttle via rAF: keep a ref for scrollTop, request animation frame to setState at most once per frame. Or use react's useTransition for the visible-range update (low priority).
issues_found.verification: Chrome DevTools performance: scroll for 2s; FPS stays at 60.

issues_found.location: Inner div with transform but no position
issues_found.title: Translated content scrolls with the container
issues_found.explanation: The inner div is NOT position: absolute. When the parent scrolls, the entire flow scrolls, AND the translateY adds to that scroll, so visible items shift twice as fast as the user scrolls.
issues_found.root_cause: transform on a flow-positioned child doesn't anchor it.
issues_found.fix: Use position: absolute; top: 0; left: 0; right: 0; on the translated container so the transform places it inside the sized spacer.
issues_found.verification: Scroll; visible items align with scrollbar position.

issues_found.title: Repeats animation on same index
issues_found.explanation: If the parent component re-renders with the same scrollToIndex value, the effect doesn't re-run, BUT if it passes a NEW reference (e.g. number literal in some odd case), or if itemHeight changes, the smooth scroll restarts in the middle of a user scroll, causing a fight.
issues_found.fix: Compare against last requested index in a ref; skip if same. Cancel any in-flight smooth scroll if user starts scrolling (listen for user scroll input).
issues_found.verification: User scrolls during programmatic smooth-scroll; user wins.

issues_found.title: Component re-renders if items array reference changes even if contents don't
issues_found.explanation: Parents that map data each render produce a new items array reference. The VirtualList rerenders even though its content is identical. For lists of 10k items the slice + map of visible window is fast, but combined with #1 this can become hot.
issues_found.fix: React.memo around VirtualList with a shallow check. Parent should memoize items.
issues_found.verification: Profiler: VirtualList renders only on actual data change.

issues_found.title: Fixed itemHeight only
issues_found.explanation: This virtualizer breaks for variable-height items (very common in real lists with multi-line text). Document this constraint OR plan for measured sizes.
issues_found.fix: Document. For variable height use a library or extend with ResizeObserver per item.
issues_found.verification: N/A.

issues_found.title: Smooth scroll across 10k items is very slow
issues_found.explanation: Browser smooth-scroll across hundreds of viewport heights can take seconds and feel laggy. Common UX: jump directly if distance > 5 viewports, smooth-scroll if closer.
issues_found.fix: Conditional behavior: 'smooth' if within ~5 viewports else 'auto'.
issues_found.verification: Scroll from index 0 to 9999; jumps immediately.

issues_that_arent_issues: Using transform for offset is the right idea (composited). Cleanup of scroll listener is correct.
broader_pattern: Virtualizers are deceptively hard. react-virtual/tanstack-virtual and react-window/react-virtualized have years of edge-case fixes around variable height, scroll restoration, and frame budget. Rolling your own only pays off for a simple fixed-height case AND when you measure the bundle save.
what_makes_this_review_senior: Catching the position+transform layout bug is hands-on CSS experience. The rAF throttle isn't an opinion - it's a known fix for the React-scroll-thrash pattern. The reviewer also flags the UX disaster of smooth-scrolling across thousands of items, which only someone who has shipped a virtualizer would think to mention.

scenario: A modal component that locks body scroll while open
submitter_context: Reusable Modal. Locks body scroll, restores on close. Uses createPortal.
looks_reasonable_because: Backdrop click closes, click inside doesn't
review_summary: Multiple modals stacked will clobber each other's scroll-lock state. The cleanup is wrong (assigns '' even when it shouldn't), the modal traps clicks but not focus or Esc, and the e.stopPropagation hack masks the real layering problem. On iOS Safari, overflow:hidden alone does not lock scroll - you need position:fixed gymnastics.

issues_found.title: Multiple modals stomp each other's body lock
issues_found.explanation: If Modal A is open and Modal B opens on top, then closes, Modal B's effect sets overflow=''. But Modal A is still open and expects body locked. Modal A's effect doesn't rerun because its `open` didn't change. Result: scroll unlocked while a modal is still up.
issues_found.root_cause: Each modal independently writes a shared global.
issues_found.fix: Use a reference-counted body-lock manager that tracks how many components want it locked.
issues_found.verification: Open two modals; close inner; outer still locks body scroll.

issues_found.title: Does not lock scroll on iOS Safari
issues_found.explanation: iOS Safari ignores overflow:hidden on body for momentum scroll. The body still scrolls underneath the modal, which is jarring. The standard fix is position:fixed; top:-scrollY; and restoring on close.
issues_found.fix: On lock: save window.scrollY, set body to position:fixed; top:-scrollY; width:100%. On unlock: restore styles AND window.scrollTo(0, savedScrollY).
issues_found.verification: Test on iOS Safari (real device or simulator); body does not scroll.

issues_found.title: Keyboard users can't dismiss the modal
issues_found.explanation: WCAG requires modals to close on Esc. Currently only backdrop click closes.
issues_found.fix: useEffect to add window.addEventListener('keydown', e => e.key === 'Escape' && onClose()) while open.
issues_found.verification: Open modal, press Esc, modal closes.

issues_found.title: Tab moves focus to elements behind the modal
issues_found.explanation: Without trapping, Tab can move focus into the obscured page below the backdrop. Screen-reader users may not even know they've left the modal. WCAG 2.4.3 (focus order) failure.
issues_found.fix: On open: store activeElement, focus first focusable in modal, intercept Tab/Shift-Tab to cycle within modal. On close: restore focus to original activeElement.
issues_found.verification: Open modal, press Tab repeatedly; focus stays inside modal.

issues_found.title: stopPropagation breaks unrelated handlers
issues_found.explanation: stopPropagation prevents the click from reaching ANY ancestor, including analytics click-tracking attached to document, modal-stack managers, or React's own event system in edge cases. Better: check e.target === e.currentTarget in the backdrop handler.
issues_found.fix: On backdrop: onClick={(e) => { if (e.target === e.currentTarget) onClose(); }}. Drop the inner stopPropagation.
issues_found.verification: Analytics tracker on document still receives clicks inside the modal.

issues_found.title: Missing dialog semantics
issues_found.explanation: Screen readers do not announce this as a modal. Needs role="dialog" aria-modal="true" and aria-labelledby pointing to the title.
issues_found.fix: Add the attributes; require a title id from caller or derive via useId.
issues_found.verification: VoiceOver announces 'dialog'.

issues_that_arent_issues: createPortal to body is fine (assuming SSR is handled elsewhere). Conditional return null is correct.
broader_pattern: Modals are the #1 a11y/state-leak component in any codebase. Always use a primitives library (Radix Dialog, Reach Dialog, headless-ui) unless you specifically need to roll your own AND have a11y review. If rolling your own, ref-count body lock GLOBALLY, never per-instance.
what_makes_this_review_senior: The reviewer ref-counts the body lock instead of just adding a useEffect cleanup - that single insight prevents a class of subtle production bugs that only manifest when modals are stacked. They also know the iOS scroll-lock gotcha by heart (savedScrollY trick), which is field-experience knowledge, not documentation knowledge.

scenario: A typed useFetch hook with generic response typing
submitter_context: Typed wrapper around fetch. Caller specifies the response shape via generic; we get full intellisense.
review_summary: This hook gives consumers FALSE confidence: r.json() returns unknown but the cast to Promise<T> hides that the response is never validated. A server returning {name: 123} crashes downstream renderers. There's also no cancellation, no status-code check, no error.name filtering, and the catch typing assumes Error but fetch rejects with TypeError or AbortError.

issues_found.title: Type assertion lies about runtime shape
issues_found.explanation: r.json() resolves to any (or unknown in stricter setups). The cast as Promise<T> tells TS to trust it, but at runtime the response is whatever the server sent. A backend bug or schema drift produces undefined fields and downstream .map/.toFixed crashes with confusing stacks far from the fetch.
issues_found.fix: Accept a Zod/Valibot schema parameter and runtime-validate the body. Type T is INFERRED from schema, not asserted.
issues_found.verification: Set server to return wrong shape; hook returns error state with a clear message instead of crashing render.

issues_found.title: setState on unmounted component / wrong url
issues_found.explanation: If url changes (or the component unmounts) while a request is in flight, the old request resolves and setState fires on a stale component or with stale data, possibly OVERWRITING a newer request. Race condition.
issues_found.fix: Create AbortController in effect, pass signal to fetch, abort in cleanup. Also ignore stale resolves.
issues_found.verification: Rapidly change url; final visible state matches the latest url.

issues_found.title: No HTTP status check
issues_found.explanation: fetch resolves on 4xx/5xx, only network errors reject. A 500 with HTML body crashes r.json(). A 401 returning {error: 'unauthorized'} is parsed as T and silently misclassified as success.
issues_found.fix: Check r.ok; if not, throw a typed HttpError with status.
issues_found.verification: Server returns 500; state becomes error with status 500.

issues_found.title: Lying about catch type
issues_found.explanation: In TS, catch parameter type can only be any or unknown. Annotating as Error is a structural fiction. Real errors include AbortError (DOMException), TypeError (network), and your HttpError (custom). Pattern-match instead.
issues_found.fix: catch (err) { if (!(err instanceof Error)) throw err; if (err.name === 'AbortError') return; ... }.
issues_found.verification: Type-check: error: Error annotation removed; manual narrowing performed.

issues_found.location: useFetch with url as cache key
issues_found.title: No deduplication or caching
issues_found.explanation: Two components rendering useFetch('/api/users/1') simultaneously make two requests. On every remount, refetch. For a production app this multiplies request volume and forces backend changes that wouldn't otherwise be needed.
issues_found.fix: Use react-query / SWR. If rolling your own, keep a Map<url, Promise<T>> with TTL.
issues_found.verification: Render two components with same url; network tab shows one request.

issues_found.title: idle never used in practice
issues_found.explanation: The effect immediately sets loading, so idle is unreachable in real renders. Either drop idle from the union or expose a manual trigger mode (useLazyFetch).
issues_found.fix: Drop 'idle' OR add a `manual` option.
issues_found.verification: N/A.

issues_that_arent_issues: Discriminated union is a great pattern for async state. setState in .then chain is fine.
broader_pattern: TypeScript at the network boundary is theatre unless paired with runtime validation (Zod/Valibot/io-ts). Many teams ship 'fully typed' fetch wrappers that crash in production when the server changes. Validate at the boundary, then trust types inside.
what_makes_this_review_senior: The reviewer's first instinct on a 'fully typed' fetch wrapper is to look for runtime validation - that's the experienced eye. They also frame the discussion (TypeScript at the boundary is theatre without runtime validation) which is the kind of architectural taste that drives team standards, not just file-level fixes.

scenario: A form with debounced async validation for an email field
submitter_context: Adds 'is this email taken?' async validation. Debounced 300ms. Submits only when valid.
review_summary: The submit button can submit a stale-valid form: if the user types, then quickly submits before the in-flight validation finishes, emailError is still null from the previous keystroke even though the new email is taken. Also no email format check, no submit button disabled state, no submit-in-progress guard (double submit), unencoded query param, and no race-safe handling of out-of-order validation responses.

issues_found.title: Submit races the in-flight validation
issues_found.explanation: User types 'taken@x.com', debounce starts, user immediately clicks Submit. emailError is null (the previous validation passed for the empty/earlier value). Submission proceeds. Server rejects (or accepts a duplicate). The async validation result arrives moments later, useless.
issues_found.root_cause: No 'validation in progress' gate on submit.
issues_found.fix: Block submit while checking=true, OR await the validation in onSubmit before posting. Better: model emailStatus as 'idle' | 'checking' | 'valid' | 'taken' and only submit on 'valid'.
issues_found.verification: Type a taken email, immediately click submit; submission is blocked until validation completes.

issues_found.title: Out-of-order validation responses
issues_found.explanation: User types 'a@b.com' (taken), then quickly changes to 'c@d.com' (free). The first request might resolve AFTER the second due to network jitter, overwriting the correct 'free' state with stale 'taken'.
issues_found.fix: AbortController in effect cleanup. Or check that the response's email matches current state before applying.
issues_found.verification: Network throttle, type two emails fast; final state matches latest email.

issues_found.title: Unencoded user input in URL
issues_found.explanation: + in email is reserved (becomes space). 'user+tag@example.com' becomes 'user tag@example.com' server-side. Other characters can break the URL too.
issues_found.fix: Use URLSearchParams({ email }).
issues_found.verification: Test 'a+b@x.com'; server receives intact.

issues_found.title: Server takes the hit for malformed inputs
issues_found.explanation: Every keystroke (debounced) sends a request even for obviously-invalid emails like 'a'. Cheap to add `email.includes('@')` as a guard.
issues_found.fix: Validate format (zod / regex) before firing the request; only check uniqueness when format is valid.
issues_found.verification: Type 'a'; no request fires.

issues_found.location: onSubmit awaits fetch but no submitting state
issues_found.title: Double submit possible
issues_found.explanation: Clicking submit twice fires two POSTs; nothing disables the button while the first is in flight.
issues_found.fix: const [submitting, setSubmitting] = useState(false); disable button when true; reset in finally.
issues_found.verification: Click rapidly; only one POST.

issues_found.title: 500 responses crash JSON parse
issues_found.explanation: If /api/check-email returns 500 (HTML error page), r.json() throws and silently rejects via the unhandled promise. The user sees no feedback.
issues_found.fix: if (!r.ok) treat as 'unknown' (don't block submit, show 'validation unavailable').
issues_found.verification: Force 500; UX degrades gracefully.

issues_found.title: Missing Content-Type
issues_found.explanation: fetch with a string body and no Content-Type defaults to text/plain; the server likely expects application/json.
issues_found.fix: headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json' }.
issues_found.verification: Server logs show JSON parsing succeeded.

issues_that_arent_issues: Debounce via setTimeout is fine for a simple form. Clearing error on empty input is correct.
broader_pattern: Async form validation has a canonical state machine: idle -> checking -> {valid|invalid|unknown}. Bundling it into ad-hoc booleans (emailError + checking) gives you bugs at every transition. react-hook-form + zod or formik with built-in async support eliminate most of these.
what_makes_this_review_senior: The reviewer recognizes the central bug isn't just a missing await but the model: booleans can't represent the necessary states. They convert to a state machine, which is the structural fix; the other 6 issues fall out naturally from the new model. They also remember that 5xx during validation needs a 'don't block submit' UX, which is the kind of detail you only know after on-calling for a signup flow.

scenario: A useResizeObserver hook for responsive components
submitter_context: Hook to observe element size. Used in charts and layouts.
review_summary: Effect has no dependency array, so a fresh ResizeObserver is created and torn down on every render - that thrashes the GPU layer thread and can cause infinite render loops because setSize inside the observer triggers another render which makes another observer. Also no debouncing of resize events (ResizeLoop warnings), no SSR safety, and no support for ref changing.

issues_found.title: Observer recreated every render -> infinite loop risk
issues_found.explanation: Each render: cleanup disconnects old observer, new observer is created and observes ref.current. The observer fires synchronously on first observe, calls setSize, which triggers a render, which... loops if the dimensions oscillate (e.g. layout depends on size). Even when stable it's wasteful: thousands of observers/sec.
issues_found.fix: useEffect(..., [ref]). The ref object is stable, so effect runs only when ref changes (which is essentially once).
issues_found.verification: Log observer creation; should be 1, not N per second.

issues_found.title: 'ResizeObserver loop completed with undelivered notifications'
issues_found.explanation: Setting state synchronously inside an observer callback can cause layout changes that trigger another resize before the browser finishes the current frame. Browsers throttle this with the famous 'ResizeObserver loop' warning, which crashes some monitoring tools (Sentry treats it as an error).
issues_found.fix: Schedule the update via requestAnimationFrame or use a deduping check: only setSize if dimensions actually changed.
issues_found.verification: Sentry/console; no ResizeObserver loop warnings.

issues_found.title: Crashes during SSR
issues_found.explanation: ResizeObserver is undefined on the server (Node). If the hook is called during SSR (Next.js, Remix), it throws ReferenceError.
issues_found.fix: Move new ResizeObserver inside the effect (effects only run client-side), or check typeof window !== 'undefined' at the top of the effect.
issues_found.verification: SSR a page using the hook; should not throw.

issues_found.title: N components = N observers
issues_found.explanation: Each consumer creates a separate ResizeObserver. Browsers limit to ~100 observers per origin reliably; beyond that performance degrades. For pages with many cards/charts, a SHARED observer dispatching to subscribers scales better.
issues_found.fix: Module-level singleton ResizeObserver with a Map<Element, Set<Callback>>; each hook subscribes/unsubscribes.
issues_found.verification: Profile with 200 instances; one observer, not 200.

issues_found.title: First render returns wrong size
issues_found.explanation: Consumers using size on first render get (0, 0). For charts this often causes a flash of zero-size rendering. Use useSyncExternalStore with a getSnapshot that reads getBoundingClientRect synchronously when possible.
issues_found.root_cause: State-based size with effect-time first measurement.
issues_found.fix: useSyncExternalStore + sync getSnapshot from element.getBoundingClientRect().
issues_found.verification: Render a consumer; first render has real size, not 0.

issues_found.title: Doesn't accept callback ref
issues_found.explanation: Many components pass callback refs, not ref objects. The hook signature locks users into the object form.
issues_found.fix: Accept Element | null directly OR return a callback ref to attach: `const [ref, size] = useResizeObserver();`.
issues_found.verification: Code review.

issues_that_arent_issues: Reading contentRect width/height is correct (vs borderBox which is also valid depending on need). disconnect on cleanup is correct.
broader_pattern: ResizeObserver hooks shipped 'in 20 lines' are how you get the cursed ResizeObserver loop in your monitoring. The right approach pools observers and dedupes updates. Many libraries (react-use, use-resize-observer) have these baked in - prefer them over rolling your own.
what_makes_this_review_senior: The reviewer catches the missing dep array (everyone catches that) but also explains WHY it's catastrophic (observer churn + loop warning) and proposes pooling - which is the kind of optimization you only think of after profiling a page with 200 cards. The ResizeObserver loop warning vs Sentry detail is field-experience knowledge that prevents a noisy alerts inbox.

scenario: A top-level error boundary that catches render errors and shows a friendly fallback
submitter_context: Wraps the app root to prevent white-screen-of-death on crashes. Logs to Sentry.
looks_reasonable_because: Uses both static method and componentDidCatch
review_summary: Once tripped this boundary stays broken forever - no way to retry. Wrapping at the router level means a single component crash takes down the WHOLE app rather than a route segment. The boundary also won't catch async errors, event handlers, or SSR errors, but the team likely expects it to. Sentry integration uses the wrong API and won't attach React-specific tags.

issues_found.location: render() returns fallback with no reset
issues_found.title: No recovery mechanism
issues_found.explanation: Once hasError=true the user is stuck on the fallback forever. Route changes don't reset state because the boundary doesn't unmount. The user has to hard-refresh.
issues_found.fix: Pass a reset() to the fallback. Reset state on route change (key the boundary on location.pathname) or via an explicit 'Try again' button.
issues_found.verification: Trigger error, click 'Try again' (or navigate); app recovers.

issues_found.title: One crash = whole-app white screen
issues_found.explanation: A single broken card or widget shouldn't tank the entire UI. Boundaries should be nested - one at root for catastrophe, but also one per route segment, and optionally around expensive widgets so they fail independently.
issues_found.fix: Layer boundaries: root, per-route, per-section. Each renders its own scoped fallback.
issues_found.verification: Force a crash in a sidebar widget; main content still renders.

issues_found.title: Sentry ErrorBoundary helper unused
issues_found.explanation: @sentry/react ships Sentry.ErrorBoundary which auto-tags with React component info, integrates with replay, and adds reset capability. Hand-rolling loses those tags AND the documentation around fingerprinting.
issues_found.fix: Use Sentry.ErrorBoundary (or compose with their withErrorBoundary HOC). If you must roll your own, pass info.componentStack as `contexts: { react: { componentStack } }` for proper tagging.
issues_found.verification: Sentry issue shows React component stack as a structured field, not just extra.

issues_found.title: Implicit promise: 'this catches everything'
issues_found.explanation: Error boundaries do not catch errors in event handlers, async code, setTimeout, or SSR. The team naming this 'ErrorBoundary' at root suggests they expect global protection; document the limits OR layer with a window.onerror / unhandledrejection logger.
issues_found.fix: Add window.addEventListener('error', ...) and 'unhandledrejection' handlers that report to Sentry. Document what the boundary does and doesn't catch.
issues_found.verification: Throw in an event handler; Sentry still captures it (via global listener).

issues_found.title: componentStack can be null in React 19
issues_found.explanation: React 19's owner stacks change componentStack format and may emit null in some configurations. Code that assumes it's a string will crash inside componentDidCatch, which itself silently swallows.
issues_found.fix: Guard: info.componentStack ?? '(no stack)'.
issues_found.verification: Trigger error in a context where componentStack is null; no secondary crash.

issues_found.title: Static fallback can't show the error
issues_found.explanation: A ReactNode fallback can't render error details (e.g. for dev). Accept a function `(error, reset) => ReactNode` for richer fallbacks.
issues_found.fix: Accept render-prop fallback.
issues_found.verification: Pass a fallback that uses error message; renders correctly.

issues_that_arent_issues: Class component is still the only way to do an error boundary in React (as of 19, no hook equivalent yet). Both static method and componentDidCatch is correct.
broader_pattern: Error boundaries are part of an error STRATEGY, not a single component. Production-grade apps need: layered boundaries, async error reporters, reset on navigation, and proper observability tagging. A single 'ErrorBoundary' at root creates false confidence.
what_makes_this_review_senior: The reviewer thinks about the SHAPE of error handling, not just one boundary. Layered boundaries, reset on navigation via key, and explicitly documenting what boundaries DON'T catch shows experience triaging real production crashes. The Sentry-specific note (use the official wrapper, attach to contexts.react) is the kind of detail you only know if you've looked at your Sentry inbox.

scenario: A SkeletonLoader component used while data loads in a card list
submitter_context: Adds skeleton placeholders to reduce perceived load time. Animated shimmer with Tailwind.
looks_reasonable_because: Replaces with real data when ready
review_summary: The skeleton has different dimensions from the real card, so when data arrives the layout shifts (CLS - Core Web Vital regression). The card shell is duplicated (skeleton + real) instead of slot-composed, which guarantees they drift. Using array index as key for skeletons is fine but the same pattern is dangerous in real lists. Also no prefers-reduced-motion support and no aria-busy.

issues_found.location: Skeleton heights (h-6 + h-4 + h-4) vs real card (h3 + p)
issues_found.title: Cumulative Layout Shift on swap
issues_found.explanation:
The skeleton title is h-6 (24px), real h3 is whatever browser default + font. Skeleton body lines are h-4 each (32px total), real

wraps to N lines depending on content. When data arrives, the layout shifts visibly - hurting CLS Web Vital and looking janky.
issues_found.fix: Compose: render the real card layout always, but swap children with shimmers when loading. Or use a fixed minHeight on the card.
issues_found.verification: Lighthouse CLS score; should be < 0.1.

issues_found.title: Skeleton and real card drift over time
issues_found.explanation: Any restyle to the real card (padding, border, radius) must be mirrored in SkeletonCard - they will drift. This is a classic 'two sources of truth' bug.
issues_found.fix: Single <Card> component with `loading` prop or that accepts children that themselves can be skeletons.
issues_found.verification: Code review: only one set of shell styles.

issues_found.title: Three animations running independently
issues_found.explanation: Each child element has its own animate-pulse instance running on its own clock. Visually they desync, looking like a glitch rather than a unified pulse. Performance-wise three composited animations vs one is more GPU work.
issues_found.fix: Animate the PARENT (animate-pulse on the wrapper), children inherit. Or apply animation to a single overlay layer.
issues_found.verification: Inspect: pulse is synchronized.

issues_found.title: Animation forced on motion-sensitive users
issues_found.explanation: animate-pulse runs continuously. WCAG 2.3.3 and macOS Reduce Motion expect this to be respected. Some users get headaches/vertigo from continuous animations.
issues_found.fix: Wrap class with `motion-safe:animate-pulse` (Tailwind variant). Without it the static gray is fine.
issues_found.verification: Enable Reduce Motion in OS; no pulse.

issues_found.title: No aria-busy / aria-live for screen readers
issues_found.explanation: Screen readers announce nothing during load. Better: aria-busy="true" on the container, optional aria-live="polite" message 'Loading cards'. Then on data arrival, swap content and reader announces the new content.
issues_found.fix: Add aria-busy on the wrapper while loading; provide an off-screen status message.
issues_found.verification: VoiceOver announces 'busy' or 'loading'.

issues_found.title: undefined-as-loading is a weak signal
issues_found.explanation: `items === undefined` conflates 'not yet loaded' and 'no data'. If the API returns undefined intentionally for empty results, the UI shows skeletons forever.
issues_found.fix: Accept explicit loading prop, OR use a discriminated union: { status: 'loading' } | { status: 'success'; items } | { status: 'empty' }.
issues_found.verification: API returns no items; UI shows 'empty' state, not skeletons.

issues_that_arent_issues: Using array index as key for the static skeleton list (length 5) is fine because items don't reorder. The card list itself uses it.id, which is correct.
broader_pattern: Skeletons exist to PREVENT layout shift. If yours causes shift, you've inverted the cost-benefit. The right architecture is one card component with a loading mode, not two parallel components. Always include prefers-reduced-motion and aria-busy.
what_makes_this_review_senior: The reviewer connects skeletons to their actual purpose (preventing CLS) and recognizes the implementation works against that purpose. The architectural critique - one card component, two states - prevents the next drift bug instead of fixing this one. Catching prefers-reduced-motion and aria-busy reflects baseline a11y rigor that distinguishes shipped-to-real-users from demo code.

scenario: A useInterval hook for ticking a clock display
submitter_context: Replaces setInterval-in-effect duplication. Caller passes callback and delay.
review_summary: This is the Dan Abramov useInterval, but it still has the render-time ref mutation (concurrent-unsafe in React 19), no drift correction, and the interval keeps ticking when the document is hidden (battery drain and pointless renders). It also doesn't account for delay changing back to null mid-flight before the cleanup runs.

issues_found.title: Render-phase ref mutation
issues_found.explanation: Same issue as the debounce hook earlier. Under React 19 Strict Mode (double-invocation) and concurrent rendering (discarded renders), assigning to a ref during render leads to subtle inconsistencies. React's docs explicitly call this out as unsafe.
issues_found.fix: Move into useEffect: useEffect(() => { cbRef.current = callback; });. Or use useEffectEvent (experimental) for stable handler reference.
issues_found.verification: Strict Mode mounted twice; cbRef points to the latest stable callback.

issues_found.title: setInterval drifts on slow main thread
issues_found.explanation: For a clock, setInterval doesn't compensate for callback execution time or main-thread blocking. A 1000ms interval becomes 1050+ms when the page is busy, and the clock visibly skips. For long-running tabs the drift accumulates.
issues_found.fix: Use a self-scheduling setTimeout that computes nextDelay = delay - (Date.now() % delay). Or for a clock specifically, align to the next second boundary.
issues_found.verification: Run for 1 minute; clock displays each second exactly once.

issues_found.title: Background tab continues ticking
issues_found.explanation: Chrome throttles setInterval in background tabs to ~1Hz, so a 100ms timer effectively pauses, but a 1000ms clock keeps firing - draining battery and triggering renders. document.visibilityState should pause the interval.
issues_found.fix: Listen to visibilitychange; on hidden, clear interval; on visible, re-arm AND tick immediately to catch up.
issues_found.verification: Switch tabs; CPU drops to near-zero in DevTools.

issues_found.location: delay null pauses but callback freezes
issues_found.title: Toggling delay from number -> null -> same number doesn't restart cleanly
issues_found.explanation: When delay flips to null the effect tears down. When it flips back to the SAME number value the effect re-runs (delay changed reference-wise) and a new interval starts. But the FIRST tick happens after `delay` ms - this can be jarring if user expects immediate tick on resume.
issues_found.fix: On resume, optionally fire immediately by calling cbRef.current() before setInterval.
issues_found.verification: Pause and resume; first tick fires immediately.

issues_found.title: Even when hidden, parent renders cascade
issues_found.explanation: setNow every second triggers a render of the Clock and any subscribed parents. If Clock is inside a context provider, that provider's children may rerender unnecessarily.
issues_found.fix: Render the clock as a sibling, not nested inside heavy contexts. Or use a global time store with selectors.
issues_found.verification: Profiler: only Clock rerenders per tick.

issues_that_arent_issues: `delay: number | null` as a pause signal is fine. Clearing interval in cleanup is correct.
broader_pattern: useInterval is the second-most-common 'foot-gun' hook after useEffect itself. Drift, hidden-tab handling, and concurrent ref safety are the three areas to audit any time you see it. For clocks specifically, consider whether the consumer can derive time on render rather than driving state.
what_makes_this_review_senior: The reviewer recognizes a canonical pattern (Dan Abramov's useInterval) but doesn't treat it as untouchable - they call out the concurrent-rendering hazard that was added in React 18+. The drift correction and visibility integration are the kind of polish you only add after debugging a 'why does our clock skip on slow pages' or 'why is our laptop battery dying' ticket.

scenario: A Breadcrumbs component that renders a path from URL params
submitter_context: Reads :slug and :tab from the route, displays them in a breadcrumb trail.
review_summary: This is an XSS vector. A URL like /products/%3Cscript%3Ealert(1)%3C%2Fscript%3E lands the params into dangerouslySetInnerHTML and executes attacker JavaScript with the victim's session. There's no reason to use dangerouslySetInnerHTML here at all - just render the text. Also missing aria-current, missing security considerations on the href, and useParams typing is wishful.

issues_found.title: Reflected XSS from URL
issues_found.explanation: URL params are attacker-controllable. decodeURIComponent + innerHTML injection = stored/reflected XSS depending on context. An attacker emails a link with a script-tag payload; victim clicks; script runs as the user (steal session, exfiltrate data).
issues_found.root_cause: Unnecessary HTML injection of untrusted input.
issues_found.fix: Just render the string: {decodeURIComponent(slug)}. React escapes by default.
issues_found.verification: Navigate to /<script>alert(1)</script>/x; no alert.

issues_found.title: Open redirect / javascript: scheme
issues_found.explanation: If slug is javascript:alert(1) or //evil.com, the href becomes a javascript link or protocol-relative redirect to attacker domain. Browsers may interpret slug-as-pathname here, but a clever encoding bypasses that on some routers.
issues_found.root_cause: Direct interpolation of untrusted input into href.
issues_found.fix: Validate slug shape (alphanumeric + dashes only) before using. If using Link from react-router, prefer to({pathname:...}) which constrains shape.
issues_found.verification: slug = 'javascript:alert(1)'; clicking does nothing harmful.

issues_found.title: Generic type lies about runtime
issues_found.explanation: useParams returns Record<string, string | undefined> regardless of generic. The TS annotation suggests slug/tab are always strings but they may be undefined - which the code happens to handle via truthy check, but lulls future maintainers into a false sense of completeness.
issues_found.fix: Don't annotate; use the inferred Record<string, string | undefined> and narrow explicitly.
issues_found.verification: Type-check: slug typed as possibly undefined.

issues_found.title: A11y: breadcrumbs need aria-label
issues_found.explanation: Multiple <nav> elements on a page (main nav, breadcrumbs, footer nav) need aria-labels to be distinguishable by assistive tech.
issues_found.fix: <nav aria-label="Breadcrumb">. Last item should have aria-current="page".
issues_found.verification: VoiceOver announces 'Breadcrumb navigation'.

issues_found.title: react-router already decodes; double-decode mangles
issues_found.explanation: react-router decodes URL params before exposing them via useParams. Calling decodeURIComponent again is a no-op for valid input but throws URIError on input containing a stray % (e.g. '100%'). The error crashes the breadcrumb (and possibly the page).
issues_found.fix: Don't decode; useParams already gives decoded values. Render directly.
issues_found.verification: slug = '100%25' (encoded '100%'); breadcrumb shows '100%'.

issues_found.title: Breadcrumb separator not styled / semantic
issues_found.explanation: Plain text separator is read aloud as 'slash' by screen readers, which is verbose. Use a styled span with aria-hidden, or list semantics (ol/li) with CSS-generated separators.
issues_found.fix: <ol class="flex gap-2"> with <li> items; separator via CSS or aria-hidden span.
issues_found.verification: Screen reader reads items without 'slash slash slash'.

issues_that_arent_issues: Falsy guards on slug/tab are reasonable. Tailwind classes are fine.
broader_pattern: dangerouslySetInnerHTML is a code smell in 95% of cases - if you're injecting untrusted content (URLs, params, user input), you almost always want text rendering instead. Any review that sees this should ask 'why?' first. Also: route params are untrusted user input, even when they 'look like our own URLs'.
what_makes_this_review_senior: Catching the XSS is table stakes for an experienced reviewer - what makes this senior is recognizing it's a needless dangerouslySetInnerHTML in the first place (not just escaping the input). They also catch the open-redirect via slug-in-href, the double-decode URIError crash, and the lie about useParams generics, all of which would have shipped together as a 'small breadcrumbs PR.'

scenario: A Tooltip component triggered on hover
submitter_context: Reusable Tooltip with smart positioning. Renders content next to the target.
looks_reasonable_because: Smooth opacity transitions could be added easily
review_summary: The tooltip content is ALWAYS rendered into the DOM (just hidden with opacity), so every tooltip evaluates its content prop on every render of the parent. For an icon grid with 50 tooltips containing markdown, this becomes 50 markdown renderers always live. Also no keyboard focus support, no touch support, content is rendered inside an <span> with possibly block children (HTML invalid), and no portal so overflow:hidden ancestors clip it.

issues_found.title: Content always mounted in the DOM
issues_found.explanation: `open` only toggles opacity; the content prop is rendered every render. If content is expensive (Markdown, large JSX, chart preview), that work happens for EVERY tooltip on the page on EVERY parent render - even though only one tooltip might ever be hovered.
issues_found.root_cause: Optimized for transition smoothness, ignored mounting cost.
issues_found.fix: Conditionally render: `{open && ...}`. Use a CSS transition with display none/block carefully, or render an empty placeholder when closed.
issues_found.verification: Profile a page with 50 tooltips containing Markdown; only the hovered one shows render work.

issues_found.title: No keyboard or touch support
issues_found.explanation: Keyboard users can't trigger tooltips (need focus/blur). Touch users have no hover; the tooltip is unreachable. WCAG 1.4.13 requires hover-only content to be dismissible AND keyboard-accessible.
issues_found.fix: Add onFocus/onBlur on the trigger. For touch, show on tap (toggle) and dismiss on outside tap or after a timeout.
issues_found.verification: Tab to trigger; tooltip shows. Tap on mobile; tooltip shows.

issues_found.title: Invalid HTML if children contain <button> or block elements
issues_found.explanation: Span is phrasing content; wrapping a <button>, <div>, or <a> inside causes invalid HTML in strict parsers and may break layout (block elements don't render correctly inside inline). React renders it but it's a CSS landmine.
issues_found.fix: Use a div with display: inline-block, or accept asChild pattern (like Radix) to merge with the trigger.
issues_found.verification: Pass a button as children; layout is correct, HTML validator clean.

issues_found.title: Clipped by overflow:hidden ancestors
issues_found.explanation: Common card/menu containers use overflow:hidden. The tooltip, positioned absolutely inside the trigger, gets clipped. Real tooltips render to a portal.
issues_found.fix: createPortal to document.body and use Floating UI (or computed coordinates) to position next to the trigger.
issues_found.verification: Place tooltip in a card with overflow:hidden; tooltip displays fully.

issues_found.title: Screen readers don't link tooltip to trigger
issues_found.explanation: Without aria-describedby on the trigger pointing to the tooltip's id, screen readers don't announce the tooltip when focusing the trigger.
issues_found.fix: useId for tooltip id; set role="tooltip" on tooltip; aria-describedby={id} on the trigger.
issues_found.verification: VoiceOver announces tooltip content when trigger is focused.

issues_found.title: No delay; tooltip flashes during scanning
issues_found.explanation: Tooltips that appear instantly are noisy and trigger on every accidental pass. UX convention is 300-500ms delay before showing.
issues_found.fix: setTimeout in onMouseEnter to setOpen after delay; clear on leave.
issues_found.verification: Hover briefly; tooltip doesn't flash.

issues_that_arent_issues: Opacity transition for show/hide can be a fine choice if it's the only animated property. Tailwind classes themselves are correct.
broader_pattern: Tooltips are tiny but encode a surprising amount of UX state (delay, focus support, touch, keyboard dismiss, positioning, portaling). Like menus, they're one of the canonical 'use the library' components - Radix Tooltip, react-tooltip-floating, etc. If rolling your own, copy the WAI-ARIA pattern entirely.
what_makes_this_review_senior: The 'always-in-DOM tooltip' issue is something you only feel when profiling a page with many tooltips - it's invisible in code review unless you're looking for it. The reviewer also covers keyboard, touch, portal-clipping, and ARIA in one pass, none of which the original PR thought about. Suggesting Radix or Floating UI rather than expanding the in-house version is the experienced trade-off call.

scenario: An infinite-scroll list that loads more items when the user reaches the bottom
submitter_context: IntersectionObserver-based infinite scroll for the activity feed.
review_summary: This double-fetches page 0 on mount and races multiple pages on fast scroll. Specifically: useEffect for fetch runs with page=0 on mount, AND the observer also fires with isIntersecting=true on mount because the sentinel is already in view (the empty list takes no space), causing setPage(1) before page 0 has even returned. No cancellation, no in-flight guard, no error handling, no scroll restoration on remount.

issues_found.location: useEffect fetch + IO incrementing page on mount
issues_found.title: Race: observer fires before first fetch completes
issues_found.explanation: On initial render the items list is empty, so the sentinel div is at the top of the page (in view). The IntersectionObserver immediately fires isIntersecting=true on observe, calling setPage(1). Now page jumps from 0 to 1 while page 0 is still in flight. Possibly skip page 0 entirely, or order swaps.
issues_found.fix: Track in-flight state; observer should only trigger increment when not loading AND when at least one page has loaded. Pre-fill items with a min-height so sentinel isn't already visible.
issues_found.verification: Network log shows page 0 then page 1, never simultaneous, never out of order.

issues_found.title: Out-of-order responses corrupt feed
issues_found.explanation: Even if you fix #1, slow page N response after fast page N+1 means items are appended in wrong order: [N+1 items, N items]. User sees timeline jumbled.
issues_found.fix: Tag each request with its page; on response check it matches the highest applied page + 1; otherwise drop or queue.
issues_found.verification: Network throttle; pages always appended in order.

issues_found.title: setState on unmounted component
issues_found.explanation: User navigates away mid-fetch; response fires setItems on unmounted component. React 18+ logs a noisy warning; can mask real bugs.
issues_found.fix: AbortController in effect; pass signal to fetch; abort on cleanup.
issues_found.verification: Navigate away during fetch; no console warning.

issues_found.title: Network/JSON errors silently abandoned
issues_found.explanation: If the fetch rejects or returns 500, items never update, but `done` stays false. The sentinel re-enters view (perhaps because user scrolls), observer fires, page increments, request retries silently. The user has no idea anything's wrong.
issues_found.fix: Catch errors; set an error state; show 'Couldn't load more, retry?' UI; disable observer until retried.
issues_found.verification: Force 500; UI shows error, observer paused.

issues_found.title: Page incremented while sentinel is intersected continuously
issues_found.explanation: After page 0 loads, if the sentinel is STILL in view (small page size), IO doesn't refire isIntersecting:true (it stays true) - so this might NOT trigger. But if you scroll-up then back down, it refires. The result is non-deterministic depending on scroll velocity.
issues_found.root_cause: Continuous intersection isn't a fresh trigger.
issues_found.fix: Threshold + rootMargin (e.g. rootMargin: '200px') and track last triggered page to avoid double increment.
issues_found.verification: Manual test: scroll fast; one increment per scroll-past.

issues_found.title: No deduplication across StrictMode mounts
issues_found.explanation: In dev with React 19 Strict Mode, the fetch effect mounts twice on first render. With useEffect cleanup, the first fetch is abandoned but still completes server-side - the network tab will look weird. Document or filter dev-mode aborts.
issues_found.fix: AbortController fixes this side-effect-wise; document for dev clarity.
issues_found.verification: Dev mode; abort signals dropped pages cleanly.

issues_found.title: Returning to the feed scrolls back to top
issues_found.explanation: User scrolls to page 5, clicks an item, comes back - items list is empty (re-fetching from 0), scroll position lost. Real feeds use a cache or persist scroll position.
issues_found.fix: Use react-query with caching, or store items + scrollY in a module-level store keyed by route.
issues_found.verification: Navigate away and back; feed position restored.

issues_that_arent_issues: Using IntersectionObserver instead of scroll-position math is correct. Tracking done flag prevents endless pagination.
broader_pattern: Infinite scroll is one of those features that LOOKS simple - 'just observe a sentinel' - but encodes pagination, ordering, cancellation, error handling, and scroll restoration. react-query's useInfiniteQuery handles all of these correctly. The team should evaluate adopting it before extending this hand-rolled version.
what_makes_this_review_senior: The mount-time race (empty list -> sentinel in view -> instant double-fetch) is invisible to most engineers because it only triggers when the feed has no initial content. The reviewer catches it, but more importantly recognizes infinite scroll as a 'use the library' feature - react-query's useInfiniteQuery exists specifically because every hand-rolled implementation has these bugs.

scenario: A util module re-exporting helpers and date formatting for the app
submitter_context: Centralizes utilities. Re-exports everything from lodash for convenience.
looks_reasonable_because: Mix of date-fns and moment because some legacy code uses moment
review_summary: This file blows up the bundle. `import _ from 'lodash'` pulls in the whole library (~70kb minified). Re-exporting moment AND date-fns AND lodash means consumers pull all three even if they only use one helper. `import * as Icons from 'react-icons/fa'` is a 1.5MB import (every Font Awesome icon). The 'centralize utilities' pattern is exactly the antipattern modern bundlers want you to avoid.

issues_found.title: Pulls entire lodash bundle
issues_found.explanation: `import _ from 'lodash'` imports the namespace; bundlers can't tree-shake from a namespace import that's then re-exported. Even if a consumer only uses chunk, they get the full ~70kb minified.
issues_found.fix: Import individual functions: `import chunk from 'lodash/chunk';` Or use lodash-es with named imports: `import { chunk } from 'lodash-es';`.
issues_found.verification: Bundle analyzer: lodash size drops to per-function size.

issues_found.title: 1.5MB of icon definitions
issues_found.explanation: react-icons/fa is the entire Font Awesome icon set as JS exports. Namespace import defeats tree-shaking; even with named imports the package recommends importing from `react-icons/fa` specifically so SOME tree-shaking works. Star-importing into a namespace pulls everything.
issues_found.fix: Named imports only: `import { FaUser, FaHome } from 'react-icons/fa';`. Or use lucide-react which is per-icon by default.
issues_found.verification: Bundle analyzer: only used icons present.

issues_found.title: Moment is deprecated and ~280kb
issues_found.explanation: moment.js is officially in maintenance mode, has a huge bundle, includes all locales by default, and is mutable. Mixing it with date-fns means TWO date libraries in the bundle.
issues_found.fix: Migrate moment calls to date-fns or dayjs (2kb). Remove moment entirely. Use codemod if many call sites.
issues_found.verification: `grep moment` returns no results in app code; bundle size drops.

issues_found.title: Barrel files break tree-shaking
issues_found.explanation: Even with named exports, barrel files cause many bundlers to evaluate all exports' modules (and their side effects) when ANY symbol is imported. Worst case: importing `chunk` from utils evaluates moment, lodash, date-fns, and icons.
issues_found.fix: Import directly from the source module (utils/date.ts, utils/array.ts) - skip the barrel. Mark utils package as sideEffects: false in package.json to help bundlers prove safety.
issues_found.verification: Import single helper; analyzer shows only that module evaluated.

issues_found.title: structuredClone exists
issues_found.explanation: Modern browsers (and Node 17+) have structuredClone built in. Lodash cloneDeep is no longer necessary for typical objects.
issues_found.fix: Replace with `structuredClone(obj)`. For deeply-nested with Maps/Sets it handles them too.
issues_found.verification: Tests pass; lodash usage decreases.

issues_found.title: Lodash debounce is ~3kb; native alternative is 10 lines
issues_found.explanation: For a simple debounce, the lodash version (which supports leading/trailing/maxWait) is overkill. A 10-line inline version saves space if those options aren't used.
issues_found.fix: Write a small debounce util; or import lodash.debounce directly if needed.
issues_found.verification: Bundle analyzer.

issues_found.title: any leaks through debounce
issues_found.explanation: `...args: any[]` widens to any in TS and weakens type safety of debounced wrappers.
issues_found.fix: Use Parameters<F> and proper generics.
issues_found.verification: Type-check: callers' types preserved.

issues_that_arent_issues: Mixing date-fns and moment temporarily during migration is reasonable - but it should be a migration, not a permanent state.
broader_pattern: Modern bundlers (Vite, Next 14+, esbuild) tree-shake AGGRESSIVELY when given clean imports. Barrel files, namespace imports, and 'utils' god-modules systematically defeat that. The fix is cultural: per-file imports, no central barrel, and bundle-analyzer in CI to catch regressions.
what_makes_this_review_senior: The reviewer doesn't just call out individual heavy imports - they identify the BARREL FILE as the root anti-pattern that creates the problem and prevent its return. Recommending sideEffects: false and a CI bundle-size check shows preventive thinking: this PR will be the last 'utils centralization' attempt, not the first of many.
