scenario: A reusable Select component with custom keyboard support
submitter_context: Custom select to match design tokens. Drops the native <select> styling limitations.
review_summary: This 'select' is keyboard-broken: no arrow keys to navigate options, no typeahead, no Escape, no Home/End. Items are <li> with onClick, unreachable by keyboard, with no roles. It also doesn't close on outside-click and doesn't manage focus. The 'space to open' interaction will silently fail in form contexts because Space inside a focused div inside a form may trigger button activation upstream. Native <select> works correctly with zero of these bugs - the team is reinventing a broken wheel.

issues_found.title: Cannot navigate options with keyboard
issues_found.explanation: Native <select> uses ArrowUp/Down (and typeahead) to move between options. This component only opens on Enter/Space; options can only be selected by mouse. Keyboard users are stuck.
issues_found.fix: Implement listbox keyboard pattern: ArrowDown moves activeIndex; Enter selects; Escape closes; Home/End jump; printable chars do typeahead.
issues_found.verification: Open select via Enter, ArrowDown through options, Enter to select.

issues_found.title: Options not keyboard-operable
issues_found.explanation: <li> isn't focusable or activatable by keyboard, no role, no aria-selected. Screen readers don't recognize it as an option in a listbox.
issues_found.fix: role='listbox' on the ul; role='option' aria-selected={value===o.value} on each li; manage active item via aria-activedescendant.
issues_found.verification: VoiceOver announces 'listbox, 5 options'.

issues_found.title: Open menu never closes if user clicks elsewhere
issues_found.explanation: Click outside doesn't close the menu. User has to click the trigger again. Native <select> closes on blur.
issues_found.fix: document.addEventListener('mousedown', closeIfOutside) while open. Or use Radix/headless-ui Select.
issues_found.verification: Open menu, click elsewhere, menu closes.

issues_found.title: Tab leaves the open menu instead of cycling options
issues_found.explanation: Tab moves focus to next form field on the page, leaving the open menu visible. Awkward and confusing.
issues_found.fix: On open, focus list; intercept Tab to close (matching native).
issues_found.verification: Open, Tab; menu closes, focus moves to next field.

issues_found.title: Won't submit with form
issues_found.explanation: Real <select> participates in form submission. This div-based version doesn't, so `<form>` submission ignores it. If the team is replacing <select>, callers who use uncontrolled forms break.
issues_found.fix: Render a hidden <input type='hidden' name={name} value={value} /> alongside.
issues_found.verification: Place inside a form, submit; FormData contains the value.

issues_found.title: Why not just style native <select>?
issues_found.explanation: Modern CSS (appearance: none + accent-color) and the new <selectlist> proposal cover most styling needs. Native <select> has all the keyboard/a11y/touch behavior for free. Custom selects exist for cases where you need rich content in options (icons, multi-line); this PR doesn't show that need.
issues_found.fix: Reconsider. If custom needed, base on Radix Select or react-aria's useSelect.
issues_found.verification: PR description should state why native won't work.

issues_found.title: O(n) lookup per render
issues_found.explanation: With small option lists this is fine. For 1000+ options it adds up. Consider a Map for value -> label.
issues_found.fix: useMemo a Map for lookup if N > 100.
issues_found.verification: N/A for small lists.

issues_that_arent_issues: Tailwind classes themselves are fine. Returning a controlled component via value/onChange is the right API.
broader_pattern: Custom select is one of the most common 'overengineered, broken on accessibility' components in React codebases. Native <select> works everywhere, supports keyboard/touch/screen readers/mobile pickers automatically, and has years of browser engineering behind it. The bar for replacing it should be high.
what_makes_this_review_senior: Senior reviewers reflexively question whether to replace a native control - native <select> is the most well-engineered piece of UI in browsers. Catching the missing form-submission integration is the kind of bug that's invisible until the PR ships and a user reports 'my dropdown doesn't save'. The listbox ARIA pattern (aria-activedescendant + aria-selected) is well-trodden but rarely implemented correctly.

scenario: A useWebSocket hook to keep a live connection to the server
submitter_context: Replaces a global ws singleton. Each consumer of the hook gets the connection state.
review_summary: Each useWebSocket call opens its OWN socket - 5 components using the hook means 5 connections to the same server. Strict Mode mount/unmount/remount cycles the socket immediately on mount (visible in network tab, may trigger server reconnect alerts). The messages array grows unbounded and is held in component state, leaking memory the longer the page is open. No reconnect on disconnect, no error handler, no JSON parsing.

issues_found.title: One socket per hook call instead of one shared socket
issues_found.explanation: If five components use useWebSocket('/api/feed'), five separate WS connections open. The server sees five connected clients per user. For chat/feed/notifications this is a real backend load and behavior multiplier.
issues_found.fix: Module-level connection manager: one socket per URL, reference-counted with subscribers. Hook subscribes/unsubscribes.
issues_found.verification: Render 5 components with same URL; backend sees 1 connection.

issues_found.title: React 19 Strict Mode opens, closes, reopens the socket
issues_found.explanation: Strict Mode in dev double-invokes effects (mount, cleanup, mount). The WS opens, closes immediately, reopens. The server's connection log fills with churn and per-client setup costs (auth, subscription) double.
issues_found.root_cause: Effect without dedupe at the connection layer.
issues_found.fix: Connection pool with delayed cleanup (e.g. close 1s after last subscriber unsubscribes) absorbs the dev-mode bounce.
issues_found.verification: Dev mode; only one connection in network tab.

issues_found.title: Unbounded message array
issues_found.explanation: Every message appended forever. For a chat or live feed this leaks memory and degrades render performance as the array grows. Component-state-as-message-log is the wrong shape.
issues_found.fix: Cap the buffer (e.g. last 200), OR don't store messages in this hook at all - emit them to a callback and let the consumer decide storage (database, indexed buffer, etc.).
issues_found.verification: Send 10000 messages; memory and renders stable.

issues_found.title: Socket closes; hook never reconnects
issues_found.explanation: On network blip the socket closes; status stays 'closed' forever. Most WS use cases need exponential-backoff reconnect.
issues_found.fix: On unexpected close (not user-initiated), retry with backoff. Cap retries, surface 'disconnected' UX.
issues_found.verification: Drop network; socket reconnects on reconnection.

issues_found.title: Last subscriber wins
issues_found.explanation: If for some reason multiple effects access the same ws (e.g. with #1 fixed via shared connection), assigning to ws.onmessage = ... overwrites previous handlers. Use addEventListener('message', ...) instead.
issues_found.fix: Always use addEventListener with named handlers; remove on cleanup.
issues_found.verification: Two subscribers; both receive messages.

issues_found.location: setState in handlers without staleness check
issues_found.title: setState fires after cleanup runs in close handler
issues_found.explanation: In Strict Mode, the closing socket's onclose may fire after cleanup; setState on unmounted updates produces warnings (React 18+ allows it but it's noisy).
issues_found.fix: Track a `mounted` ref; gate setState calls.
issues_found.verification: No 'setState on unmounted' warnings.

issues_found.title: Doesn't parse JSON
issues_found.explanation: Most WS use cases send JSON. Storing raw strings shifts that work to every consumer.
issues_found.fix: Accept a generic schema; parse and validate (Zod) inside the hook.
issues_found.verification: N/A.

issues_that_arent_issues: Re-running on url change is correct. Tracking status in state is reasonable.
broader_pattern: WebSocket connections are SHARED resources, not per-component. Hooks that own a connection per call multiply backend load invisibly. Production-grade WS clients use a connection manager pattern (one socket per URL, subscribers, reconnect, backoff) like phoenix.js or socket.io.
what_makes_this_review_senior: The reviewer correctly identifies that the architecture is wrong (per-call connections vs pooled), not just the implementation. Reference-counted pool with delayed close is the production pattern that absorbs Strict Mode bounce. They also catch the unbounded messages array - a common slow leak that only manifests on hour-long sessions, the kind found by support tickets, not unit tests.

scenario: A copy-to-clipboard button with success feedback
submitter_context: Tiny utility button. Copies a value to the clipboard and shows 'Copied!' for 2s.
review_summary: Three production failure modes: clipboard API silently rejects in insecure contexts (HTTP, sandboxed iframes), Safari requires the API call inside a SYNCHRONOUS user-gesture handler (an await before writeText breaks the user-activation chain on iOS), and the setTimeout leaks if the component unmounts before it fires. Also no error feedback - users have no idea why it didn't work.

issues_found.title: Fails in insecure contexts and older Safari without fallback
issues_found.explanation: navigator.clipboard is only available on https or localhost. In HTTP environments (dev preview servers, staging on raw IP, embedded iframes from non-secure parents) the API is undefined or rejects. Users see no feedback - silently broken.
issues_found.fix: Feature-detect; fall back to document.execCommand('copy') with a hidden textarea + selection. Yes, execCommand is deprecated but still works as a fallback.
issues_found.verification: Test on http://10.x.x.x; copy still works.

issues_found.title: Awaiting before clipboard call breaks user-activation chain on iOS Safari
issues_found.explanation: iOS Safari requires clipboard writes to happen synchronously inside the user gesture handler. If you await anything before writeText (or even compute value asynchronously), Safari rejects the call as 'not in a user gesture'.
issues_found.root_cause: Async wrapping consumed the gesture token.
issues_found.fix: Call writeText FIRST, await its promise without doing other async work before it. Make sure value is available synchronously. If you must compute async, use the Clipboard API with ClipboardItem and a Promise that resolves the data; Safari accepts that pattern.
issues_found.verification: Test on iOS Safari (real device); copy succeeds.

issues_found.title: setTimeout leaks on unmount
issues_found.explanation: If the component unmounts within 2s of click, setTimeout still fires and setState on unmounted component (warning + small leak retaining the closure). Also, rapid clicks stack timers and shorten/elongate the feedback duration unpredictably.
issues_found.fix: Store timer ref; clear on unmount and on new click before setting again.
issues_found.verification: Click then unmount; no warnings.

issues_found.title: User has no idea when copy fails
issues_found.explanation: If the promise rejects (permission denied, insecure context), the button silently does nothing. The user repeatedly clicks, confused.
issues_found.fix: try/catch; show an error state ('Couldn't copy') or fall back to a 'press Ctrl+C' instruction with selection.
issues_found.verification: Deny clipboard permission; UI shows fallback message.

issues_found.title: Screen reader doesn't announce 'Copied!'
issues_found.explanation: The text change happens but isn't announced. Add aria-live='polite' to the visible label or use a sr-only aria-live region.
issues_found.fix: {copied ? 'Copied' : ''} alongside the visual feedback.
issues_found.verification: VoiceOver announces 'Copied' when clicked.

issues_found.title: Button clickable mid-action
issues_found.explanation: Rapid clicks during the 2s feedback window can stack setTimeouts. Disable briefly.
issues_found.fix: disabled={copied}.
issues_found.verification: Click 10 times fast; one copy operation, stable feedback.

issues_that_arent_issues: Using the Clipboard API as the primary path is correct. 2s feedback duration is reasonable.
broader_pattern: Clipboard, Permissions, Service Worker - browser APIs that look simple are loaded with platform-specific gotchas (Safari user-gesture rules, insecure-context restrictions). 'Works on my Chrome' is not shipping. Test matrix should include iOS Safari and an HTTP environment.
what_makes_this_review_senior: The iOS Safari user-activation gotcha is the kind of thing only people who have shipped on iOS catch - it's invisible in any test environment that isn't real Safari on a real device. The reviewer also distinguishes 'secure context only' from 'iOS gesture' as two SEPARATE issues, not lumping them as 'clipboard is finicky'. The execCommand fallback is dated but pragmatic.

scenario: A drag-and-drop reordering hook for lists
submitter_context: Drag-and-drop reorder for a kanban column. Uses native HTML drag-and-drop events.
review_summary: Reordering inside onDragOver fires CONSTANTLY (multiple times per second per pixel of mouse movement), causing item shuffle on every micro-move and an unusable janky drag. There's no keyboard support, no touch support, no a11y, no announcement of moves, and no commit-on-drop signal to the server. Items reorder optimistically with no way to know when 'real' drop happened.

issues_found.title: Reorders fire on every dragover event
issues_found.explanation: dragover fires continuously while the cursor is over a target (many times per second). Setting items state on each fire causes a re-render storm and the dragged item visually jumps around or oscillates. Smooth drag-and-drop fires the reorder ONCE per target boundary crossing, not per event.
issues_found.fix: Only swap when crossing the midpoint of the target item; track lastSwapTarget to avoid repeated swaps over the same item.
issues_found.verification: Drag slowly across items; one swap per crossing.

issues_found.title: No touch support; broken on mobile
issues_found.explanation: HTML5 drag-and-drop has no touch event equivalents. On mobile, the entire feature is unusable. Many libraries (dnd-kit, react-beautiful-dnd) abstract over pointer events for cross-device support.
issues_found.fix: Use dnd-kit / Pragmatic drag-and-drop / @atlaskit/pragmatic-drag-and-drop. They handle touch + pointer + keyboard uniformly.
issues_found.verification: Drag on mobile; works.

issues_found.title: Keyboard users can't reorder
issues_found.explanation: draggable=true is ignored by keyboard. WCAG requires keyboard alternative for drag operations - typically Space to pick up, Arrow keys to move, Space to drop.
issues_found.fix: Implement keyboard drag (space + arrows) OR provide buttons (move up/down) alongside drag. Libraries provide this.
issues_found.verification: Tab to item, Space, ArrowDown, Space; item moves.

issues_found.title: Reorder optimistic with no server commit
issues_found.explanation: User reorders, switches tabs, comes back - reorder lost (or worse, server doesn't have it but UI does, then a refetch overrides). Need to POST the new order to the server and reconcile on success/error.
issues_found.fix: On drop (onDragEnd if not cancelled), POST the new ordering. Show error if it fails (rollback or retry).
issues_found.verification: Reorder, refresh; order persisted.

issues_found.title: Screen readers don't know items moved
issues_found.explanation: Items reorder silently. Screen readers need a polite announcement: 'Item B moved up. Position 2 of 5.'
issues_found.fix: aria-live='polite' region; update with the move description on each swap.
issues_found.verification: VoiceOver announces position changes.

issues_found.title: User has no idea where drop will land
issues_found.explanation: Intermediate reorder is one UX pattern; another (and often clearer) is to show a placeholder/indicator where the item WILL land. The mid-drag reorder can confuse users into dropping at the wrong place.
issues_found.fix: Render a placeholder/insertion line; only commit reorder on drop.
issues_found.verification: UX review.

issues_found.title: Should also set setData and cursor
issues_found.explanation: Some browsers (Firefox) need at least one setData call for drag to start. Cursor feedback (e.dataTransfer.dropEffect = 'move' on dragover) hints to the OS.
issues_found.fix: Add e.dataTransfer.setData('text/plain', id) in onDragStart; set dropEffect in onDragOver.
issues_found.verification: Drag in Firefox; works smoothly.

issues_that_arent_issues: draggable={true} on each item and visual opacity feedback are standard patterns.
broader_pattern: Drag-and-drop is one of the canonical 'this looks easy until you ship it' features. Touch, keyboard, screen reader, persistence, conflict resolution, and visual feedback all matter. dnd-kit + a thin wrapper is the lowest-cost way to get a complete implementation; rolling your own pays off only if you have very specific needs and an a11y budget.
what_makes_this_review_senior: The reviewer correctly identifies that reordering during dragover (not just at boundary crossings) is the visible bug, but more importantly says 'use the library' for the structural reasons (touch, keyboard, a11y). They also flag the missing persist-to-server flow, which is exactly the bug that ships and only gets reported by users after a week of confusion.

scenario: A weather widget on a marketing page that shows the current time and a 'good morning/afternoon/evening' greeting based on the user's local timezone.
submitter_context: PR description: 'Adds a personalized greeting widget to the homepage hero. Uses the browser's Date API to render the right greeting (Morning/Afternoon/Evening) and the current local time. Should improve perceived freshness.'
review_summary: This will hydration-mismatch in production for every SSR'd request because the server's clock (UTC by default) won't match the client's local time. The 'fix-it-up' useEffect doesn't help React's hydration check — React will throw 'Text content does not match server-rendered HTML' on the initial mount, blow away the SSR tree, and re-render client-side. That defeats SSR for this section.

issues_found.location: useState(formatTime()) and getGreeting() called during render
issues_found.title: Hydration mismatch from time-dependent server render
issues_found.explanation: During SSR, the server calls new Date() in its timezone (typically UTC). The client calls new Date() in the user's timezone. Server renders 'Good evening. It's 11:43 PM' while the user (in PST, 4 hours behind) sees 'Good afternoon. It's 7:43 PM'. React's hydration sees the mismatch, logs a warning, and throws out the server tree. The user briefly sees the server text, then it flickers to client text, then it updates per second.
issues_found.root_cause: Calling Date during render makes the render output depend on wall-clock time, which differs between server and client.
issues_found.fix: Render a placeholder during SSR and on first client render; only show the live time/greeting after mount. Or use suppressHydrationWarning narrowly on the time-only span (acceptable for this exact case since user-visible flicker is harmless and we don't want to hide content).
issues_found.verification: Disable JS in browser - the page should render without the time/greeting block. With JS, the time/greeting should appear post-mount without a hydration warning in console.

issues_found.title: Re-renders the entire widget every second
issues_found.explanation: Every 1s the state updates, the entire widget re-renders. On marketing pages with many widgets, this can churn React. More critically: the interval fires even when the page is in a background tab. After 1 hour in a background tab, that's 3600 unnecessary renders.
issues_found.root_cause: Per-second updates aren't required for a greeting widget; minutes are. And background-tab updates are wasted.
issues_found.fix: Update on the next minute boundary, not per second. Pause when the page is hidden via document.visibilitychange.
issues_found.verification: In dev tools, switch to a different tab for 1 minute. Switch back: the widget should reflect the new minute without having burned 60 ticks while hidden.

issues_found.title: Hardcoded en-US locale ignores user's locale
issues_found.explanation: On a marketing page targeting global users, hardcoding en-US means a Japanese user sees '11:43 AM' instead of '11:43' (24h convention). This violates the i18n contract a marketing page implicitly makes with international visitors.
issues_found.root_cause: Hardcoded locale where intent was 'user's locale'.
issues_found.fix: Use toLocaleTimeString(undefined, { hour: 'numeric', minute: '2-digit' }) — undefined locale picks up the user's locale.
issues_found.verification: In dev tools, change browser language to ja-JP, reload, observe 24h format.

issues_found.title: Greeting boundaries are culturally American
issues_found.explanation: Many cultures don't bucket 0-12-18 as morning/afternoon/evening. In Spanish, 'buenos días' extends past noon. In some Asian cultures, 'good morning' extends to ~10 AM only. For a global marketing site, this is a localization bug.
issues_found.fix: Use Intl.DateTimeFormat's 'dayPeriod' or accept that the greeting is in English and the boundaries are approximate. If localizing, externalize boundaries per locale.
issues_found.verification: Acceptance depends on how localized the marketing site is. If the page is en-only, accept as-is and document. If localized, fix.

issues_found.title: Stale interval not paused when tab hidden
issues_found.explanation: setInterval runs even when the page is hidden, wasting CPU and battery on background tabs. Modern browsers throttle setInterval in background tabs to ~1 minute, but the renders still happen at the throttled cadence.
issues_found.root_cause: No coordination with Page Visibility API.
issues_found.fix: Use document.visibilitychange to pause/resume; or use requestAnimationFrame which is auto-paused.
issues_found.verification: Open in a tab, measure CPU in chrome://performance, switch to a different tab for 5 min, return. CPU graph during hidden period should show near-zero for this widget.

issues_that_arent_issues: The setState initial value calling formatTime() is fine in principle - useState lazy initializer or eager works here. The 'lazy initializer' optimization doesn't matter for a string. Not a real issue.
broader_pattern: Any value derived from new Date() during render creates a hydration mismatch under SSR. The general rule: render-time values must be deterministic across server and client, OR you must render a placeholder during SSR and update post-mount. This applies to Date, Math.random, document/window access, and any locale-dependent formatting.
what_makes_this_review_senior: The hydration mismatch issue is invisible in development (typically same machine, same TZ as the dev server). It only manifests when production servers (UTC) serve users in non-UTC timezones. A junior reviewing this would test in dev, see it work, approve. A senior recognizes that new Date() during render is one of the canonical SSR/CSR mismatch patterns and demands a placeholder-during-SSR approach. The other catches (background tab CPU, hardcoded locale) come from production-experience patterns: 'this widget will run on millions of devices including idle background tabs and non-en-US users'.

scenario: A 'recent activity' widget on a dashboard that fetches activity for the current user + the filters selected in the sidebar.
submitter_context: PR description: 'Wires up the recent activity widget to refetch when filters change. Uses useEffect to re-fetch on dependency change. Tested manually with filter changes.'
review_summary: The dependency array is correct in intent but useFilters likely returns a new object reference on every render, causing this effect to refetch on every render of the parent. Combined with the missing race-condition guard, the user will see flicker between stale and current data when filters change rapidly. There's also no error path and no cleanup.

issues_found.title: filters reference instability triggers refetch every render
issues_found.explanation: useFilters likely returns the filters object from a store (Zustand, Redux, Context). If the store doesn't use shallow equality or memoization, filters is a new object reference each render even when contents haven't changed. The effect re-fires on every parent render. On a dashboard, this means dozens of API calls per minute for a widget that should fetch once.
issues_found.root_cause: Object reference equality in dep arrays is a common React footgun. Stores must guarantee referential stability for unchanged state.
issues_found.fix: Either: (a) Use useMemo to stabilize a serialized version: `const filterKey = useMemo(() => JSON.stringify(filters), [filters])` and depend on filterKey; (b) Fix at the store level: use useFilters((state) => state.filters, shallow) for Zustand or similar; (c) Replace with React Query: const {data} = useQuery({queryKey: ['activity', user.id, filters], queryFn: ...}).
issues_found.verification: Add console.log inside the effect. Open the dashboard and trigger any unrelated state change (mouse hover causing parent re-render). The effect should NOT log unless filters actually changed.

issues_found.title: Race condition: stale fetch can overwrite fresh
issues_found.explanation: User toggles filter A → request A starts. User immediately toggles filter B → request B starts. Request B returns first (faster). Component shows B's data. Request A returns. setActivity(A.items) overwrites B's data. User sees stale results.
issues_found.root_cause: Async effect with no cancellation; setState fires regardless of whether the effect that started the fetch is still 'current'.
issues_found.fix: Use AbortController, check on resolve whether the effect's cleanup ran, OR use a fetching library that handles this (React Query, SWR). The cleanest fix for this codebase: React Query.
issues_found.verification: Rapidly toggle filters 5+ times. Watch Network tab for the requests. Verify the displayed data matches the LAST filter selection, not whatever returned last.

issues_found.title: Unhandled rejection on network error
issues_found.explanation: If the fetch fails (network down, 500 response), the .then chain rejects, leaving loading=true forever and the user stuck on a skeleton. Worse, the rejection is unhandled, which in production can crash the page via window.onunhandledrejection handlers tied to error tracking.
issues_found.fix: Add .catch handler that sets an error state and stops loading. Or use try/catch in an async function inside the effect.
issues_found.verification: Block /api/activity in network conditions, observe that the widget shows an error state rather than infinite skeleton.

issues_found.title: JSON.stringify(filters) in URL query string
issues_found.explanation: Embedding JSON in a URL query string isn't URL-safe (special chars need encoding) and produces long URLs. Some CDNs/proxies cap URL length at 2KB. With deeply nested filter objects, requests fail in production with 414 URI Too Long.
issues_found.root_cause: Mixing URL semantics with JSON serialization.
issues_found.fix: Use POST with body for complex filters, or URLSearchParams with flat key=value pairs.
issues_found.verification: Test with a filters object containing 50+ entries; verify request succeeds.

issues_found.title: Loading flicker on rapid filter changes
issues_found.explanation: On each filter change, loading flips to true, skeleton renders, fetch completes, loading flips to false. For rapid filter toggling, the user sees skeleton flicker. Could be addressed with optimistic UI or debouncing.
issues_found.root_cause: Loading state is binary; UX needs are tri-state (initial loading vs background refresh).
issues_found.fix: Show previous data with a subtle 'updating' indicator instead of full skeleton on refetch. React Query's isFetching vs isLoading distinction handles this.
issues_found.verification: Toggle filters quickly; verify the list doesn't flash a skeleton, instead shows a subtle loading indicator.

issues_found.title: No empty state
issues_found.explanation: activity defaults to []. If the user has no activity matching filters, the user sees nothing. Not broken but bad UX.
issues_found.fix: Add explicit empty state: 'No activity matches your filters'.
issues_found.verification: With filters that match no records, verify the user sees a helpful message.

issues_that_arent_issues: Using useState for activity rather than useReducer is fine - the state transitions are simple. Inline arrow function in .then is fine here - no perf concern. The 'no useCallback wrap for fetch' is not an issue because there are no dependents.
broader_pattern: useEffect with an object/array dependency is almost always a footgun. The dependency-array contract requires referential equality, but objects from stores or props rarely have it without explicit memoization. The patterns that work: (1) depend on primitive values (strings, numbers), (2) memoize complex deps explicitly, (3) use React Query/SWR which handle key serialization correctly. Custom fetch-in-effect almost always has these bugs - prefer a fetching library.
what_makes_this_review_senior: Recognizing that 'filters' as an object dependency is a near-guaranteed bug under any store implementation that doesn't memoize. Juniors trust the dependency array; seniors verify referential stability. Also: catching the race condition without seeing it happen requires recognizing the pattern (async effect + no cancellation = always a race). The CDN-URL-length issue is from production scars - this exact bug bricked a prior team's dashboards when filter objects grew. The push to React Query isn't a 'preference' - it's recognizing that this widget needs at least 5 things React Query gives you free.

scenario: A comment system on a blog allows users to post comments with limited markdown (bold, italic, links). The frontend renders the parsed comments.
submitter_context: PR description: 'Adds comment rendering with markdown-it. Supports bold, italic, links. Sanitized via DOMPurify before render. Includes link target=_blank for external links.'
looks_reasonable_because: DOMPurify is the canonical sanitization library
Markdown-it has html: false to disable raw HTML
Links get target=_blank rel=noopener for security
Author shown as text, not innerHTML
review_summary: The DOMPurify call is correct but the .replace AFTER sanitization re-introduces an injection vector. The regex transforms `<a ` blindly, which means an attacker crafts content that DOMPurify accepts as safe but the regex mutates into something unsafe. Author rendering is also missing an XSS guard if the comment object isn't entirely trusted.

issues_found.title: Post-sanitization mutation re-introduces XSS
issues_found.explanation: DOMPurify sanitizes output to a known-safe string. The .replace then runs string-level regex on that output, which doesn't understand HTML semantics. An attacker can craft a comment that DOMPurify allows but contains text like 'click here' - DOMPurify already stripped dangerous attributes but if the replace inserts target=_blank before sanitization's understanding of where attributes end, the resulting HTML can have malformed attribute boundaries that browsers parse permissively. Even when DOMPurify strips javascript: URLs, the regex's blind transformation can produce attribute-order changes that re-expose bypasses in some browser parsers.
issues_found.root_cause: Treating sanitized HTML as a string and editing it. Sanitized HTML should be treated as immutable post-sanitization.
issues_found.fix: Use DOMPurify's ADD_ATTR hook to add target/rel to all <a> elements DURING sanitization, not after. DOMPurify.sanitize(html, { ADD_ATTR: ['target', 'rel'] }) plus a hook that sets them.
issues_found.verification: Run the OWASP XSS Filter Evasion Cheat Sheet payloads through the new code path. Verify no script execution in console.

issues_found.title: linkify auto-wraps URLs - including dangerous schemes
issues_found.explanation: linkify: true makes markdown-it convert text like 'javascript:alert(1)' into a link. While DOMPurify strips that scheme, the combination of linkify + post-sanitization mutation means there are more paths for the .replace bug above to be exploited.
issues_found.root_cause: Auto-linking expands the attack surface; should be opt-in only for http(s).
issues_found.fix: Configure markdown-it linkify to only http/https: md.linkify.set({ fuzzyProtocol: false }) and md.linkify.add('javascript:', null).
issues_found.verification: Type 'javascript:alert(1)' as a comment. Verify it renders as plain text, not as a link.

issues_found.title: Author rendering trusts c.author is safe text
issues_found.explanation: React's JSX escapes strings by default, so {c.author} is safe in terms of script injection. BUT if c.author contains long strings with no spaces (a single 5000-char username), it breaks layout, can DoS rendering, or be used for social engineering (mimic admin names). Also if author can contain unicode RTL override characters (U+202E), it can mimic other content visually.
issues_found.fix: Add length limit and unicode normalization at display time. Or enforce at the backend (preferred). At display: clip to ~40 chars, normalize unicode to NFKC, strip RTL/bidi override chars.
issues_found.verification: Create an account with 'normaluser<RTL OVERRIDE>moc.live@bob' as username, verify display is sanitized.

issues_found.title: Comment content lacks CSP nonce or strict source
issues_found.explanation: Even with DOMPurify, defense-in-depth dictates that Content Security Policy should restrict inline scripts. The current page presumably allows inline styles for the markdown rendering. CSP policy review is part of any dangerouslySetInnerHTML usage.
issues_found.root_cause: CSP not enforced at the page level.
issues_found.fix: Add a CSP header with script-src 'self' and style-src that allows the design system's classes. Document the CSP policy.
issues_found.verification: Review CSP report endpoint after deploy; verify no violations on comment-heavy pages.

issues_found.title: Shared mutable parser instance
issues_found.explanation: MarkdownIt instances are mutable. If anywhere else imports this and calls md.use() to add a plugin, all comment rendering changes for everyone. Bug surface from action-at-a-distance.
issues_found.fix: Wrap in a factory function or freeze the instance after configuration: Object.freeze isn't enough but at least don't export the raw instance.
issues_found.verification: Search the codebase for other imports of MarkdownIt; ensure no shared instance mutation.

issues_found.location: missing rel attribute on internal links
issues_found.title: rel=noopener only on the replaced links, not all anchors
issues_found.explanation: DOMPurify can let internal/relative links through with no rel attribute. The .replace adds target=_blank rel=noopener to all anchors, which is the bug above. The correct config should be: external links get target=_blank rel=noopener noreferrer; internal links stay default; both get bounds checking on href to prevent open-redirect.
issues_found.fix: DOMPurify hook: inspect href; if external, add target=_blank rel='noopener noreferrer'; if internal/relative, don't add target; if href starts with javascript: or data:, strip the anchor entirely (DOMPurify likely does this already).
issues_found.verification: Comment with 'see [our /about](/about) page' renders as in-tab link. Comment with 'visit [example.com](https://example.com)' renders as new-tab with rel=noopener noreferrer.

issues_that_arent_issues: DOMPurify is correctly chosen - it's the right library. The author rendering via JSX text node (not innerHTML) is correct for the script-injection axis. The lack of memoization on renderComment isn't a real issue for typical comment counts (<100 per page).
broader_pattern: Sanitization output is contract: the sanitizer guarantees safety only if you don't mutate the output. Any string mutation post-sanitize voids the warranty. The same principle applies to: not parsing-then-mutating-then-stringifying JSON, not splitting-then-rejoining sanitized URLs, not lower-casing-then-string-replacing CSS. Sanitized output is immutable. Achieve transformations via the sanitizer's hooks/config.
what_makes_this_review_senior: The .replace-after-sanitize issue is subtle - the code 'looks careful' (DOMPurify is there!) but post-mutation defeats it. A junior sees DOMPurify and approves. A senior thinks 'sanitization output is a contract' and reviews everything that touches the output. The unicode-override attack on author names is from real incidents (phishing emails using RTL chars). Insisting on CSP, frozen parser instance, length limits - these come from production XSS post-mortems where any one missing control was the entry point.
